replies.author: @JohnnyJustice777
replies.text: ​​@newworldman2112 Why i said side character is because then you can give a more superficial account. Ill summarise her whole story: A young genius with a troubled past and lofty ambitions, made a deal with a demonic being to get her friend back. Synopsis of her character don't sound too bad, but then they gave her too much screentime and plot points and now her character is a mess. If she were a side character, then they can shift the focus to someone else . Then they can also cut a lot of her horrible scenes. They could easily put this whole story as a small story arc in some other hero's series. Like, they could ask her why she is serving Mephisto and she could say that she made a mistake. Its a more of a cautionary tale and shows how manipulative Mephisto can be. Yup, they could have basically had a midnight sun's storyline, with her being a small part of the story and having an eventual redemption arc turning against Mephisto. Making her a main character meant they had to spend more time highlighting her innumerable flaws, which made her even worse.

replies.text: ​@Ragin_Sage Tony Stark didn’t just have arrogance, he earned it through raw genius and extreme situations. People forget he was basically a one-man army with a wrench and scrap metal in a cave, while bleeding out. The ego was just the surface — underneath was someone who constantly delivered when the stakes were life or death. By the time he’s building weapons from Home Depot gear in Iron Man 3, it’s not about the tools — it’s about the mind behind them. Stark didn’t need superpowers. He was the power.

replies.author: @Randomperson-u7f
replies.text: ​@quazymoro6237 You’re right that Tony Stark was the “Merchant of Death” — and yes, he did cause a lot of pain through his weapons and reckless arrogance. But isn't that exactly why his arc is so powerful? Redemption doesn't start with perfection — it starts with acknowledgment and change. The cave wasn’t just a physical prison, it was a moral awakening. And the fact that he didn't ignore his past but actively fought to undo it — at great cost — is what gives Iron Man his heart. You mention that his villains were born from his mistakes — true. But unlike most heroes, Tony confronts the consequences of his own actions head-on. He takes responsibility in a way that few others in the MCU do. So here’s a question: would his story be more compelling if he had been a saint from the beginning? Or is it more meaningful because he started from the bottom morally and chose to climb? People remember Iron Man not because he was perfect — but because he grew, stumbled, and still chose to lay down his life for a world he once profited from. That’s not short-sighted — that’s the point.

replies.author: @stephenvargas5806
replies.text: @quazymoro6237actually not accurate at all. It’s not that he didn’t care until he was hurt by his own weapons. Tony genuinely believed his weapons were only being sold and used by America and her allies which he was ok with him because his father “helped win WW2” as he tells Kristine at the very beginning of the movie. He’s proud of the legacy of his company serving the US. While he may be naive and a bit cynical he did actually believe his weapons were doing good by protecting America. It’s not until he sees his life work being sold and used by bad guys that he decides to intervene. He actually never once shows an ounce of self pity that he himself was hurt by his own bombs.  All his rationalizations are always put at the forefront the people that were hurt by the misuse of his weapons. As he said at the press conference “I saw young Americans killed by the very weapons I built to protect them.” He also tells pepper that he is trying to protect the people that he put in harms way and that’s his life now.

replies.author: @EvanOfTheDarkness
replies.text:
@toucanman No, in fact this would be a wonderful starting point for a character arc - if she had one.

Creating a very flawed unlikeable protagonist, and then dragging them though hell, to emerge as a hero is a tried and tested way to make good characters. You don't need to look to too far for examples, Ironman himself is a prime example.

That's why it's so puzzling that nothing like that happens with Riri. She starts of as unlikeable and morally bankrupt, and by the end she is still unlikable and morally bankrupt. She suffered no losses, had no self reflection, and learned no lessons. Except, you know that demonic magic is the solution to all of her problems.

If you don't redeem your villain, then she's just a villain.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
A story about how people can be corrupted and need redemption would be fine. it's difficult to tell a story about losing your soul and not understand what it means to become damnable. But they garble morality at every turn. With Ironheart, wanda and Captain Marvel, they undercut more villainous actions. Then they had media like Brave New World and Thunderbolts, which tried to be kindlier and allow people to reform. But messages about growth and reforming ring hollow from a studio that can't help but layer in moral relativism. They can't flit between forgiveness and 'what even is evil?'. They have to have a level of technique to get it to work. To use a weird metaphor, the difference between incinerating food and flambéing it is knowing what you're doing. Even good ingredients and bold ideas can still be a total mess.

Unless it's been retooled, they had the nerve to make a story about how marginalisation doesn't mean you're blameless. There are definitely points in the last 5 years when that's a bold message for the progressive audience they seem to want. The road to hell being paved with good intentions is an interesting story. But her intentions aren't that good and they were likely going to give her a 'get out of hell free' card anyway.

Within the plot, people die as a consequence of actions she's party to. That's not a minor party foul. An incorrect model of redemption allows people to do what they want, never acknowledge the gravity of their actions, and then reform without it needing to be a massive deal.

replies.author: @sigmacademy
replies.text: Technically, the Devil would, because on the one hand, it would destroy her future but also in using her as its army of agents of chaos on Earth and elsewhere - think Spawn in that regard. The thing is, the character as presented, is thoroughly unlikeable. So even if you set up a redemption arc for her, it would just be one where she realizes that she's a top-tier narcissist and everyone around her enabled her in that behavior. That would mean nobody of her "friends" in the snow is actually her friends, and the only person she can get along with a chatbot wearing the face of her dead friend, which means striking out on her own, making new friends and stop blaming the world for everything wrong in her life - taking control over the things in her life she can control and managing those optimally. You could try to explore the psychological reasons for why she's acting out like that, trying to fix the character into a half-way likeable character, by giving her a proper journey of development, but I'm not holding my breath for anything decent to come out of this studio anymore.
